nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lowland swamps, peat swamp forests, and slow-moving streams with dense vegetation and typically acidic, blackwater conditions

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared foods including flakes, pellets, and freeze-dried foods. Live and frozen offerings such as brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms tend to bring out coloration and encourage natural foraging. Occasional blanched vegetables are also accepted. Best fed small amounts 2-3 times per day.

avoidWith: very boisterous fast swimmers that may cause stress

imageCandidates.notes: Stefan Maurer, 2206x1477. Sharp full-body male in nuptial colour: vivid orange-red throat/breast, dense pearl mosaic spotting across body and fins, black lateral stripe, and pearl-spotted extended dorsal/anal fins — best diagnostic display.
NasserHalaweh, 1980x1485. Crisp lateral view of a whole male over a dark natural background, showing pearl spotting, dark midline, orange breast and trailing pelvic filaments.
h080, 3888x2592. High-res sharp side profile of a different individual with fine pearl spots, blue-tinged fins and long thread-like pelvic fin; complementary angle.

summary: A large, pearl-spotted gourami from Southeast Asia widely regarded as one of the most peaceful gouramis available. Males develop a vivid orange-red throat and extended fin filaments. Well-suited to planted community tanks.
careNotes:
The Pearl Gourami is generally considered one of the easiest and most beginner-friendly of the larger gourami species. It is commonly regarded as more peaceful than many other gouramis in mixed-species setups, though males tend to be territorial toward one another and can be aggressive toward females when conditioning for spawning — a ratio of one male to two or more females, with adequate cover, is generally advised for groups.

As a labyrinth fish (meaning it breathes atmospheric air directly, supplementing its gill respiration), it regularly surfaces to gulp air from above the waterline. A gap must be maintained between the waterline and the tank lid, and the lid should fit snugly — escape risk is moderate, as these fish may jump, particularly when startled or during spawning activity.

The species was formerly classified as Trichogaster leeri; the current accepted name is Trichopodus leerii, though the older name still appears in trade literature and on store labels. It is native to peat swamp environments in the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, and is listed as Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss. Most specimens available in the hobby are captive-bred and tend to be adaptable across a broad range of water parameters. Reported lifespans vary across sources, with estimates ranging from around 4 to 5 years on the lower end to 8 years or more in well-maintained aquariums.
breedingNotes: The Pearl Gourami is a bubble-nest builder, generally considered moderately straightforward to breed in a species tank or conditioned pair setup. Males construct nests at the water surface using air bubbles and plant material — nests can reach up to around 25 cm across. A breeding tank with no more than 15–20 cm of water depth, floating plants, and gentle air-powered filtration is commonly advised. Conditioning with live or frozen foods helps trigger spawning. Spawning typically produces 200–300 eggs; hatching occurs within roughly 20–30 hours at typical breeding temperatures. Fry become free-swimming around 4–5 days post-hatching. The male guards the nest and should be removed once the fry are free-swimming. Fry initially rely on gill respiration and are sensitive to cold air above the water surface, so keeping the air space above the waterline warm is important in the early weeks.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Specialist species profile used as primary; most complete parameter and biology data. Used as primary in place of Aquarium Co-Op, which had no dedicated species page for this entry — only a passing mention in a list article without detailed parameters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range, adult size (12 cm TL), water parameters (temp 24-28°C, pH 6.0-8.0, dH 5-19), and tank minimum length (120 cm). Confirmed family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes. IUCN Near Threatened status noted.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (30 gal / ~114 L), water parameters (temp 25-28°C, pH 6-8, hardness 5-15 dH), adult size (4-5 inches), diet, lifespan (up to 5 years), and compatibility. Minor disagreements on max temperature (28°C vs SeriouslyFish 30°C) and lifespan (5 vs 8+ years) — averaged values used.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed peaceful temperament, omnivore diet, community suitability, and approximate size (5 inches / 13 cm). No detailed parameters; used for behavioral and husbandry corroboration only.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder. Relatively straightforward to breed; see breedingNotes field for full details.
